country_signal: South Africa's SDG 8.8.2 score of 1.34 (Low quintile) reflects the historically strong COSATU union movement — right to strike, collective bargaining, and Bargaining Council system are institutionally robust. However, the effective exercise of these rights is being eroded: 33%+ unemployment means workers fear exercising rights (strike deterrent effect of replaceable workforce), labour court enforcement is slowing under government capacity constraints, and COSATU's political influence is declining as the ANC loses electoral majority. Working poverty rate of 14.3% — the highest in the dataset — reflects the gap between formal protections and lived experience. Remuneration risk: work injury coverage of 38.8% is structurally low.

country_signal: Milei administration's deregulatory labour agenda is creating acute uncertainty. The RIGI (Regime of Incentives for Large Investments) reduces mandatory collective bargaining requirements for qualifying investments. Executive pressure on CGT (Confederación General del Trabajo) and broader attempts to reduce severance costs and union power are generating political resistance (general strikes 2024). ILO SDG 8.8.2 score (3.98) may worsen if legislative reforms reduce effective union rights. Remuneration is the more acute near-term concern: peso devaluation (>50% real wage decline in 2024) is materially worsening workers' purchasing power even if nominal statutory protections remain on paper.

country_signal: Lula administration (from Jan 2023) has partially reversed Temer-era (2017) labour reform weakening. Key restoration: re-strengthening union funding (partial cotização sindical restoration), improved labour court enforcement, and increased minimum wage above inflation. SDG 8.8.2 score of 3.25 reflects moderate union rights constraints that are on an improving trajectory. Working poverty rate (0.731%) is low; PPP minimum wage ($559/month) is moderate. Government is not reversing all 2017 reforms — greater use of individual collective agreements remains — but the direction is improving.
sector_signal: Medium automation displacement risk for chemical/food process manufacturing within 12 months. Process automation (sensors, SCADA, automated filling) is incremental. Consumer-brand cost-squeeze (private-label competition, retailer margin pressure) is a live signal — cleaning products, personal care, and food sectors are all experiencing margin compression that historically flows through to labour cost pressure.

country_signal: China's SDG 8.8.2 score of 9.32 (near maximum) reflects: no independent trade unions (ACFTU is state-controlled), no right to strike, no collective bargaining in the Western sense, and systematic suppression of independent labour NGOs (most forcibly closed 2015-2019). The Xi administration's crackdown on civil society has eliminated the informal worker advocacy infrastructure that partially compensated for institutional gaps. Post-COVID labour unrest has been met with detention, not reform. Remuneration risk: minimum wage (PPP $544) is low; work injury coverage at 35.9% is among the lowest in the dataset — the gap between statutory and actual coverage is large in practice.

No institutional improvement expected within 12 months. NLRB enforcement has been active under recent Democrat-appointed boards but rule-making is subject to judicial challenge. Remuneration risk remains Low: minimum wage at PPP $1,257/month and near-zero working poverty rate (0%) maintain strong purchasing power floor. SECTOR SIGNAL: Low-medium automation displacement risk for technical/regulated production within 12 months. Regulatory compliance requirements in pharma and electronics manufacturing mandate skilled human oversight; automation replaces specific tasks within a process rather than entire roles. Worker rights in these facilities tend to be better enforced due to regulatory audit requirements.
monitoring_signals: PRO Act legislative progress; NLRB rule-making survival under judicial review; US manufacturing union density statistics; BLS contingent worker data; warehouse automation injury rate statistics

country_signal: Israel's SDG 8.8.2 score of 0.0 (exact zero) reflects strong institutional labour rights. Histadrut (General Federation of Labour) is powerful; right to strike is broadly protected; social insurance coverage near-complete (100% work injury cover at Yeruham). However, the ongoing Gaza conflict is creating acute labour market disruption: Palestinian workforce (formerly ~150k daily workers) is excluded, creating labour shortages met by importing workers from South/Southeast Asia under conditions of reduced oversight. Government capacity for labour inspection is diverted. Within 12 months, the institutional score is likely unchanged but practical worker rights for the expanding migrant worker population are deteriorating.

country_signal: Mexico's 2019 labour reform (mandating genuine union elections via secret ballot, ending protection contracts/contratos de protección) is producing measurable results: USMCA Chapter 23 Rapid Response Mechanism filings at specific plants have demonstrated enforcement traction. SNTSCRM (the union reform body) has overturned dozens of legacy protection contracts. Nearshoring investment boom is increasing demand for manufacturing workers and improving real wage bargaining power in affected corridors (Monterrey, Torreon, Tijuana). SDG 8.8.2 score of 3.81 is similar to USA but improving trajectory is more credible given structural labour market reform mandate.

country_signal: India's SDG 8.8.2 score of 5.29 reflects: Labour Codes 2020 (4 Codes consolidating 44 laws) are being rolled out state-by-state, containing provisions that ITUC identifies as reducing protections — particularly: broadened definition of 'fixed-term employment' reducing dismissal protections, extended threshold for standing orders (from 100 to 300 workers, exempting many medium enterprises from formal HR requirements), and reduced scope for unions to call strikes. PPP minimum wage of $233 is the lowest in the dataset; work injury coverage of 8.3% is critically low. These are national figures — tech/software workers in Ahmedabad and Kolkata (ZURU India) have substantially better market-based protections due to sector scarcity, but institutional rights framework remains Very High risk for the broader workforce.
